title: Recognizing the 112th Anniversary of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated.
plain_summary: This congressional resolution celebrates the 112th anniversary of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, which was started in 1913 by 22 college women at Howard University. The House of Representatives wants to honor this sorority and its more than 350,000 members for over 100 years of helping communities. The sorority has served people in the United States and around the world through programs focused on education, health, economic development, and politics. The resolution recognizes their long history of community service and positive impact.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes the one hundred and twelfth anniversary of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated, was started on January thirteenth, nineteen thirteen, by twenty-two college women at Howard University. The founders include twenty-two women with names like Osceola Macarthy Adams, Marguerite Young Alexander, and many others. This organization is a private group that does not make money. Its purpose is to help and support people through programs in local communities around the world. Delta Sigma Theta Sorority is the largest African-American Greek letter sorority. It is a sisterhood of more than three hundred fifty thousand mostly African-American college-educated women. The group has over one thousand and fifty chapters in many places including the United States, Africa, Bermuda, the Bahamas, Canada, Germany, Jamaica, Japan, the United Arab Emirates, the United Kingdom, the Virgin Islands, and the Republic of Korea. The sorority has a history of using its combined strength to promote doing well in school and helping people who need assistance. The group uses a Five-Point Program that focuses on Economic Development, Educational Development, International Awareness and Involvement, Physical and Mental Health, and Political Awareness and Involvement to help improve communities around the world. Delta Sigma Theta has shown a strong commitment to service through programs like Financial Fortitude, which helps people with personal money planning, and the Dr. Betty Shabazz Delta Academy, which helps young women in public schools prepare to be leaders. The sorority shows its global commitment to service by running the Mary Help of the Sick Mission Hospital in Thika, Kenya. It has also been recognized as a non-governmental organization by the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations and given special consultative status because of its volunteer work and humanitarian efforts around the world. Delta Sigma Theta stays active in the democratic process by holding annual Delta Days at the United Nations and Delta Days in the Nation's Capital. Current members follow in the footsteps of important leaders like Congresswoman Shirley Chisholm, Congresswoman Barbara Jordan, Secretary Marcia Fudge, Senator Carol Moseley Braun, and many others who have made important contributions to the nation's history. Delta Sigma Theta continues to make a difference in American and international communities by following the example of its founders and staying committed to sisterhood, scholarship, and service.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes the one hundred and twelfth anniversary of the founding of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated. The House also applauds and honors Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated, and all its members for more than a century of strength and distinguished service to the people of the United States and the global community.
